title: Why Space Is Completely Silent

segments.text: In space, no one could ever hear you scream.
And it's not science fiction, it's pure physics.
Sound needs air or water to travel through.
But space is an almost perfect vacuum, with nothing to carry it.
So even a massive explosion out there is totally silent.
That's why in space, no one could ever hear you scream.
Follow Curio for your daily dose of science.

description: A vacuum has nothing to carry sound, so space is perfectly silent. Follow for daily science & tech! 🌌

title: Your Phone Beats NASA's Moon Computer

segments.text: The phone in your pocket would crush the computer that landed on the Moon.
And the gap is almost impossible to imagine.
The Apollo computer had about sixty four kilobytes of memory.
Your phone has billions of times more processing power.
We literally walk around with supercomputers in our pockets.
Yet that tiny computer still guided humans a quarter million miles away.
Follow Curio for your daily dose of science.

description: The Apollo computer ran on kilobytes, yet it guided humans to the Moon. Follow for daily science & tech! 🚀

title: Why Lightning Is Hotter Than the Sun

segments.text: A bolt of lightning is five times hotter than the surface of the Sun.
And it happens in a flash too fast to see.
Lightning can heat the air to about thirty thousand degrees Celsius.
The Sun's surface is only around five thousand five hundred.
That sudden heat makes the air explode outward as thunder.
So every bolt is five times hotter than the Sun, in a heartbeat.
Follow Curio for your daily dose of science.

description: Lightning superheats the air to around 30,000°C, five times the Sun's surface. Follow for daily science & tech! ⚡

title: The Metal That Remembers Its Shape

segments.text: There's a metal that snaps back to its shape when you heat it.
And it looks almost like magic.
It's called Nitinol, a mix of nickel and titanium.
Bend it, then warm it up, and it returns to its original form.
Engineers use it in glasses frames, medical stents, and robots.
A metal that simply remembers the shape it's meant to be.
Follow Curio for your daily dose of science.

description: Nitinol is a metal alloy that snaps back to its shape when heated. Follow for daily science & tech!

title: Why You Can't Remember Being a Baby

segments.text: You spent years as a baby, but can't recall a single moment.
And scientists actually have a name for it.
It's called infantile amnesia.
Your brain's memory system is still wiring itself in those first years.
You were learning constantly, but not yet storing lasting memories.
So your earliest years were real, your brain just never saved them.
Follow Curio for your daily dose of science.

description: Infantile amnesia: your memory system isn't built yet to store those first years. Follow for daily science & tech! 🧠

title: Why Time Runs Faster On a Mountain

segments.text: Time literally runs faster on a mountain than at the beach.
And it's not a trick, it's Einstein.
Gravity actually slows down time, just a tiny bit.
The farther you are from Earth's mass, the faster your clock ticks.
GPS satellites have to correct for this every single day.
So someone on a mountain really does age a sliver faster than you.
Follow Curio for your daily dose of science.

description: Gravity slows time, so clocks tick faster higher up, and GPS has to correct for it. Follow for daily science & tech!

title: How Wi-Fi Is Basically Invisible Light

segments.text: Your Wi-Fi is basically invisible light filling your whole home.
And you're swimming in it right now.
Wi-Fi uses radio waves, which are the same thing as light.
They're just a color your eyes can't see.
Visible light, Wi-Fi, and X-rays are all one wave at different energies.
So invisible light is quietly carrying the whole internet to your phone.
Follow Curio for your daily dose of science.

description: Wi-Fi is radio waves, the same thing as light, just a color you can't see. Follow for daily science & tech! 📡

title: Why Glass Is Neither Solid Nor Liquid

segments.text: Glass isn't quite a solid, and it isn't quite a liquid.
It's something far stranger in between.
As glass cools, its atoms freeze in a messy, liquid-like pattern.
Scientists call this an amorphous solid.
It's rigid like a solid, but disordered inside like a liquid.
So glass sits in its own strange state, neither one nor the other.
Follow Curio for your daily dose of science.

description: Glass is an amorphous solid: rigid, yet disordered inside like a frozen liquid. Follow for daily science & tech!
